title: Monotonic Stack
description: A stack kept in sorted order by popping violations before pushing: solving Next Greater Element in O(n) instead of O(n²).
objectives: Understand a monotonic stack as a stack maintained in sorted order
Solve the Next Greater Element problem in O(n)
Understand why popping before pushing keeps the invariant

sections.text: 1. Recap: A Stack, With an Extra Rule

Stack solved balanced parentheses using plain LIFO order. A monotonic stack adds one rule on top: elements inside it are always kept in increasing (or always decreasing) order, enforced by popping violators before pushing anything new.
Recall the nested-loop shape from Time Complexity — checking every element against every later element is O(n²), and it repeats a lot of work: many elements end up re-scanning the same territory.
A monotonic stack maintains its elements in sorted order (increasing or decreasing) by popping any elements that would violate that order before pushing a new one.

sections.title: Tracing it (numbers = {2, 1, 2, 4, 3})
points: i=0 (2): stack empty → push index 0 → stack: [0]
i=1 (1): numbers[0]=2 is NOT < 1 → push index 1 → stack: [0, 1]
i=2 (2): numbers[1]=1 < 2 → pop 1, result[1]=2. numbers[0]=2 is NOT < 2 → push index 2 → stack: [0, 2]
i=3 (4): numbers[2]=2 < 4 → pop 2, result[2]=4. numbers[0]=2 < 4 → pop 0, result[0]=4. stack empty → push index 3 → stack: [3]
i=4 (3): numbers[3]=4 is NOT < 3 → push index 4 → stack: [3, 4]
End: result[3] and result[4] never got popped → stay -1 → final: 4 2 4 -1 -1

sections.text: Each index is pushed onto the stack exactly once, and popped at most once — across the entire algorithm, that's at most 2n total stack operations, giving O(n) overall, even though it might look like a nested loop (a for loop containing a while loop) at first glance. This 'each element does a bounded amount of total work across the whole run' reasoning is called amortized analysis.

sections.title: People in a line, watching for someone taller behind them
sections.text: Imagine people standing in a line, each wanting to know the next person behind them who's taller. As each new, taller person joins the back of the line, everyone shorter still waiting for an answer gets it immediately — resolved and removed from waiting — leaving only people taller than the newcomer still watching. That's the monotonic stack: shorter/smaller waiting elements get resolved and popped the moment something bigger arrives.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Defaulting to the O(n²) nested-loop approach when a single-pass monotonic stack solves it in O(n).
Storing values instead of indices on the stack — indices are usually needed to know WHERE in the result array to record the answer.
Getting the comparison direction backward — 'next GREATER element' pops while the stack's top is SMALLER than the current value; a 'next smaller' variant would flip that comparison.
Assuming the nested for/while loops mean O(n²) — the amortized analysis (each index pushed and popped at most once) is what actually determines the true O(n) complexity here.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a monotonic stack?
questions.answer: A stack maintained in sorted (increasing or decreasing) order, by popping any elements that would violate that order before pushing a new one.

questions.question: How does a monotonic stack solve Next Greater Element in O(n)?
questions.answer: For each new element, pop and resolve every smaller element still waiting on the stack (the new element is their next greater element), then push the current element's index.

questions.question: Why is Next Greater Element with a monotonic stack O(n) despite having a loop inside a loop?
questions.answer: Because each index is pushed at most once and popped at most once across the entire run, giving at most 2n total operations — an amortized O(n), not O(n²).

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A monotonic stack keeps its elements in sorted order by popping violators before pushing new ones. For Next Greater Element, storing indices on the stack while numbers[indices.top()] < numbers[i], each smaller element still waiting gets popped and resolved (its next greater element is the current one) before the current index is pushed. Despite the while loop nested inside a for loop, the true complexity is O(n), not O(n²), because each index is pushed and popped at most once across the whole run — an amortized analysis, contrasting with the naive O(n²) nested-loop comparison approach.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Monotonic Stack
sections.text: Monotonic stack = people in line watching for someone taller. New taller arrival resolves and removes everyone shorter still waiting. Each person waits once, resolved once — O(n) total, not O(n²).

sections.title: Next Greater Element I
sections.problemStatement: Given nums1 (a subset of nums2) and nums2 (unique elements), for each element in nums1, find its next greater element in nums2 (the first element to its right that is greater), or -1 if none exists.

examples.explanation: 4 has nothing bigger after it; 1's next bigger is 3; 2 is the last element in nums2 so nothing follows it.

hints: This is exactly this lesson's own Next Greater Element example, computed once for all of nums2, then looked up for each nums1 value.
Run the monotonic stack pass over nums2 to build a value-to-next-greater map (instead of an index-to-value result array, since nums2's values are unique).
For each value in nums1, the answer is a simple O(1) hash map lookup into that precomputed map.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each nums1 element, scan forward in nums2 from its position until a larger value is found.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: One monotonic stack pass over nums2 builds a value-to-next-greater hash map; nums1's answers are then O(1) lookups.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m) for the map and stack

sections.title: Next Greater Element II
sections.problemStatement: Given a CIRCULAR array nums, return the next greater element for every element, searching circularly (wrapping around to the start if needed).

examples.explanation: The last 1 wraps around and finds 2 at index 0... but index 1 (value 2) comes first when wrapping from index 2.

hints: This is Next Greater Element's exact monotonic stack technique, but the search needs to wrap around past the end of the array back to the beginning.
Simulate the circular wraparound by iterating index i from 0 to 2n-1, but always accessing the actual value at i % n.
The monotonic stack logic itself doesn't change at all — only the index range being walked over does.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each element, scan forward up to n-1 additional positions (wrapping with modulo) looking for a greater value.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Monotonic stack over indices 0 to 2n-1 (using i % n to access values), simulating the circular wraparound in a single O(n) pass.

sections.title: Daily Temperatures
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of daily temperatures, return an array where answer[i] is the number of days you'd have to wait after day i to get a warmer temperature, or 0 if none exists.
hints: This is Next Greater Element again, just asking for the DISTANCE to the next greater element instead of its value.
The exact same monotonic stack works — store indices, and pop-and-resolve when a warmer day is found.
The distance is simply currentIndex - resolvedIndex, since both indices are already known at resolution time.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each day, scan forward until a warmer day is found, counting the distance.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Monotonic stack of indices — pop-and-resolve with the DISTANCE between indices instead of the value.

sections.title: Online Stock Span
sections.problemStatement: Design a StockSpanner class with next(price), returning the span — the number of consecutive days (including today) ending today where the price was <= today's price.
hints: This is Daily Temperatures LOOKING BACKWARD instead of forward — 'how far back does the price stay <= today's price' instead of 'how far forward until a warmer day.'
A monotonic stack storing (price, span) pairs works: when a new price arrives, pop every stacked price that's <= it, ACCUMULATING their spans into the new price's span before pushing.
The accumulated span already accounts for everything those popped entries had absorbed earlier — no need to look further back manually.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store every price seen so far in a vector; for each new price, scan backward counting consecutive days with price <= today's.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic stack of (price, span) pairs, accumulating spans from popped entries into the new entry's span.

sections.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of bar heights representing a histogram, return the area of the largest rectangle that fits entirely within the histogram.

examples.explanation: The rectangle formed by heights 5 and 6 (width 2) gives area 10.

hints: For each bar, the largest rectangle USING that bar's height extends left and right until a SHORTER bar blocks it — 'how far can this height extend' is a next-smaller-element question in both directions.

A monotonic INCREASING stack of indices tracks bars still waiting to find their left AND right boundary: when a shorter bar arrives, every taller bar still on the stack has just found its right boundary.
When popping a bar to compute its area, its width is (current index) - (the new stack top's index) - 1 — the gap between the two bars that finally bound it, on both sides.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each bar, expand left and right as far as the height allows, tracking the resulting area.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic increasing stack of indices — a shorter bar triggers resolving every taller bar still waiting, computing each one's true width in O(1).

sections.title: Trapping Rain Water
sections.problemStatement: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map's bar heights, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
hints: Two Pointers solved this by tracking leftMax/rightMax directly — a monotonic stack solves it differently, by finding water trapped between pairs of bars as they're resolved.

A decreasing monotonic stack of indices: when a bar TALLER than the stack's top arrives, the popped bar was sitting in a 'valley' — water can be trapped above it, bounded by the new bar on the right and whatever is now the stack's top on the left.
The trapped water's height is min(leftBoundary, rightBoundary) - poppedHeight, and its width is the gap between the new top and the current index.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each index, scan left and right separately to find leftMax and rightMax from scratch.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic decreasing stack of indices: each time a taller bar arrives, it resolves the 'valley' bar just popped, computing trapped water bounded by both sides.
